output.answer: I cannot determine who the University of Miami played last week because Wikidata does not contain detailed sports schedules or recent game information. The information about college sports matchups is not available in this knowledge graph. For the most up-to-date information on University of Miami's recent games, I would need to consult sports news sites or the university's official athletic program website.
output.formatted:
I cannot determine who the University of Miami played last week because Wikidata does not contain detailed sports schedules or recent game information. The information about college sports matchups is not available in this knowledge graph. For the most up-to-date information on University of Miami's recent games, I would need to consult sports news sites or the university's official athletic program website.

SPARQL query over wikidata:
```sparql
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX wdt: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
SELECT ?opponent WHERE {
  wd:Q738258 wdt:P54 ?team .
  ?team wdt:P27 ?opponent
}
```

Using entities:
- University of Miami (wd:Q738258), also known as "The U", "UM", "U Miami (FL)", "U of M":
  - is instance of private not-for-profit educational institution
  - is instance of university
  - private university in Coral Gables, Florida, United States

Using properties:
- country of citizenship (wd:P27 as wdt), also known as "citizen of", "citizenship", "country", "(legal) nationality", "nation", etc.:
  - the object is a country that recognizes the subject as its citizen
- member of sports team (wd:P54 as wdt), also known as "club played for", "member of team", "of team", "part of team", "played for", etc.:
  - sports teams or clubs that the subject represents or represented

messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/

- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/

- owl: http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#
- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/
- pqv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value/

- psv: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/value/
- qb: http://purl.org/linked-data/cube#
- qfn: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/function#
- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/
- s: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/statement/
- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#
- v: http://www.wikidata.org/value/
- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#
- wd: http://www.wikidata.org/entity/
- wdno: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/novalue/
- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.

content.message:
I've identified the University of Miami entity in Wikidata. The most relevant one is University of Miami (wd:Q738258). 

Now I need to find information about the University of Miami's sports teams and their recent game schedules. Since Wikidata doesn't typically contain detailed sports schedules, I need to search for properties related to sports teams or games that might connect to the University of Miami.

I'll search for properties related to games or opponents that the University of Miami might have played.

tool_calls.name: search_property
tool_calls.result:
Top 10 property alternatives:
1. opposite of (wd:P461 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "opponent"), also known as "adversary", "antagonist", "contrasted to", "contrast of", "has opposite", etc.:
  - item that is in some way the opposite of this item
2. enemy (wd:P7047 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "opponent"), also known as "arch-enemy", "archenemy", "archfoe", "archnemesis", "archrival", etc.:
  - opponent character or group of this fictive character or group
3. inverse property (wd:P1696 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "opponent"), also known as "antonym", "inverse of", "opponent", "opposite of", "opposite property", etc.:
  - links a property to its inverse property (which relates the subject and object in reverse order)
4. maximum number of players (wd:P1873 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "players max"), also known as "maximum players", "max players", "number of players, maximum", "players max", "players, maximum number":
  - maximum numbers of players of a game
5. participant (wd:P710 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "player"), also known as "accused", "agent", "attendee", "belligerents", "between", etc.:
  - person, group of people or organization (object) that ... has role" (P3831). Use P1923 for participants that are teams.
6. league or competition (wd:P118 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "competition"), also known as "competition", "cup", "division", "sports league", "tournament":
  - league or competition in which team or player has played, or in which an event occurs
7. Chessgames.com player ID (wd:P1665 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "ChessGames.com ID", "Chess Games ID", "ChessGames ID":
  - identifier on the website Chessgames.com
8. Chess.com player ID (wd:P11065 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv), also known as "Chess.com player identifier":
  - identifier for a chess player on Chess.com
9. coinop.org game ID (wd:P8059 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv):
  - identifier of a video game in the coinop.org database
10. game mode (wd:P404 as wdt/p/ps/pq/pr/wdtn/psn/pqn/prn/psv/pqv/prv, matched via "player configuration"), also known as "gamemode", "gameplay", "mode", "player configuration", "player mode", etc.:
  - video game's available playing mode(s)

4. inception (wd:P571 as wdt/p, matched via "formation"), also known as "built", "commenced on date", "commencement date", "completed", "composed", etc.:

5. member of (wd:P463 as wdt/p, matched via "club"), also known as "band member of", "club", "in musical group", "is member of", "member of musical group", etc.:

6. coordinate location (wd:P625 as wdt/p, matched via "position"), also known as "co-ordinate location", "co-ordinates", "coordinates", "co-ords", "coords", etc.:

7. ranking (wd:P1352 as wdt/p, matched via "position"), also known as "peak position", "place", "placing", "position", "rank":
  - subject's ordinal position as qualitatively evaluated relative to other members of a group
8. parent organization or unit (wd:P749 as wdt/p, matched via "holding"), also known as "chapter of", "has organizational parent", "holding", "holding company", "owned by (company or organisation)", etc.:
  - parent organization or unit of an organization or unit, ... organization (Q43229) and organization unit (Q10387680)
9. funder (wd:P8324 as wdt/p, matched via "benefactor"), also known as "benefactor", "donor", "financed by", "financier", "funded by", etc.:
  - entity that gives money to a person, organization, or project for a specific purpose
10. child organization or unit (wd:P355 as wdt/p, matched via "owns"), also known as "child agency", "child company", "child institution", "child organization", "child organization unit", etc.:
  - child organization/unit of a organization/unit; for ... (P31) to distinguish org. (Q43229) and org. unit (Q10387680)
